communityVoices.quote: Single Best Dark Star Ever Bar None
one of the most driven, intense Dark Stars out there, it will always be in my tops! Billy drives the intensity and much as Jerry, and it doesn't let go.
The Playin In The Band from this show is simply RAGING! This one is a Jerry/Phil smorgasbourg.
communityVoices.attribution: bootleg43, on Playing in the Band

communityVoices.quote: this is the tour where Bob Weir's rhythm guitar becomes god-like.
You can easily imagine that this recording was given the same treatment as the tracks that wound up on Europe 72. They seem to share the same sonic footprint.

whyThisShow: This is the second night of the Europe '72 tour at Wembley Empire Pool, and the community treats it as one of the tour's anchoring performances. The Dark Star alone draws outsized attention — one reviewer called it "one of the most driven, intense Dark Stars out there" [source: 'one of the most driven, intense Dark Stars out there, it will always be in my tops! Billy drives the intensity and much as Jerry, and it doesn't let go.'], and another opened their review with the claim that it's the "Single Best Dark Star Ever Bar None" [source: 'Single Best Dark Star Ever Bar None']. The Playing in the Band also gets called out specifically, with one listener describing it as "simply RAGING" and a "Jerry/Phil smorgasbourg" [source: 'The Playin In The Band from this show is simply RAGING! This one is a Jerry/Phil smorgasbourg.']. The show is unreleased officially, but the soundboard recording is widely circulated and praised for its mix — one reviewer noted it shares "the same sonic footprint" as the tracks that made the Europe '72 album [source: 'They seem to share the same sonic footprint.'].
listenFor: The Dark Star > Sugar Magnolia > Caution sequence is the centerpiece and the reason most people seek this tape out. One reviewer mapped the arc closely, noting that "around 21 minutes things start getting creepy with a very interesting, almost circus like Pigpen buried in the background" [source: 'Around 21 minutes things start getting creepy with a very interesting, almost circus like Pigpen buried in the background.'], and that the band "turn on a dime and settle into a mellow theme that forms a smooth segue into Sugar Mag" [source: 'they turn on a dime and settle into a mellow theme that forms a smooth segue into Sugar Mag.']. The Caution that closes things out is described as "one of the best, the best from this tour in any case" [source: 'the Caution as mentioned is one of the best, the best from this tour in any case.']. Don't skip the Playing in the Band either — one listener noted that "the fellas never stop creating throughout the life of this Playin" [source: 'The fellas never stop creating throughout the life of this Playin.'].
context: April 8, 1972 was the second show of the Europe '72 tour, recorded via soundboard (identifier: gd72-04-08.sbd.giles-jeffm.2534.sbeok.shnf). The tour is one of the most documented and celebrated in Dead history, and this show has never received an official standalone release despite the tour's extensive archival treatment. Pigpen was still performing but the band was, as one reviewer put it, "slid more into Jerry's world" [source: 'the group slid more into Jerry\'s world'], with Pigpen contributing selectively rather than anchoring sets. One reviewer named this date as the top pick across the entire Europe '72 run [source: 'The best (so far) in my opinion are: 04/08, 04/11 (Newcsastle), 04/26 (Jahrhundert Halle), 05/03 (Paris), and 05/26 Lyceum, London).'].
funFact: One reviewer used the Dark Star from this show as a recruitment tool, noting that "after about 15 minutes of this Dark Star, he was totally feeling it" [source: 'after about 15 minutes of this Dark Star, he was totally feeling it.'].
headline: Wembley, April '72 — the Dark Star people keep returning to.
